title: The Dust Mite Challenge
question_text: A speck of dust is 0.000035 meters long. How do we express this in standard form?

options.feedback: It looks like you used a positive exponent. Remember, for tiny numbers (less than 1), the exponent must be negative to show we are dividing by 10.
Correct! Nice one — you’re getting the hang of this.
Standard form requires exactly one non-zero digit before the decimal point. 35 is too big! Try moving the decimal so its sits between 3 and 5
You're close! But check your 'jump' count again. How many places does the decimal actually move to get behind the 3?

hint: Count decimal jumps to the right of the first non-zero digit.
full_solution: To get 3.5 from 0.000035, the decimal moves 5 places to the right. Since it's a small number, the exponent is −5. Result: 3.5 × 10^-5..

title: The Paper Trail
question_text: The thickness of a sheet of paper is 0.0016 cm. What is its standard form?

options.feedback: Correct! Your thinking is on point.
You might have stopped the decimal too early. Count the jumps from its original spot to the right of the 1.
A negative exponent doesn't make the number negative; it just means it's a tiny decimal. Also, small numbers need negative exponents, not positive ones.
While the value is the same, this isn't 'Standard Form.' Standard form only allows one digit before the decimal (like 1.6)

hint: Move the decimal 3 places to the right to place it directly after the first non-zero digit (1).
full_solution: Moving the decimal in 0.0016 three places right gives 1.6. The exponent is −3. Result: 1.6 × 10^-3.

title: Micro-Measurements
question_text: True or False: 0.000000564 written in standard form is 5.64 × 10^-7.

options.feedback: That’s right — your instincts are strong.

options.text: False. The correct answer isnt in the options.
options.feedback: Check your count! If you jump the decimal 7 times, it lands between the 5 and the 6. That's the goal!
A positive 7 would mean a very large number (over 50 million!). For this tiny decimal, we need the negative sign
Standard form is picky! You can only have one digit (the 5) to the left of the decimal.

hint: Count the jumps from the original decimal point to the spot between 5 and 6
full_solution: To go from 0.000000564 to 5.64, the decimal moves 7 places right. This makes the exponent −7. The statement is True.

title: Binary Tiny
question_text: The diameter of a wire is 0.0000021 m. What is its standard form?

options.feedback: Correct! You’re building real confidence here.
You moved the decimal an extra step! In standard form, we stop as soon as there is one non-zero digit to the left of the decimal (2.1).
Don't forget the negative sign! Tiny decimals use negative exponents to show we are moving the decimal to the right.
You haven't moved the decimal far enough. There must be one non-zero digit (like 2) on the left side.

hint: How many jumps does it take to get the decimal between the 2 and the 1?
full_solution: Jumping 6 places right puts the decimal after the 2. Result: 2.1 × 10^-6.

title: The One-Tenth Rule
question_text: What is the standard form of 0.1?

options.feedback: Correct! Keep Going!!
1 × 10^1 is 10. For the decimal 0.1, we need a negative exponent to show it's a fraction (1/10).
Standard form requires the first number to be 1 or greater (up to 9.99...). 0.1 is too small; move that decimal!
10^0 is just 1. 1 × 1 equals 1, not 0.1.

hint: Move the decimal one place to the right to get 1.
full_solution: Moving the decimal 1 place right gives 1 × 10^-1.

title: Reverse Engineering
question_text: A lab report lists a size as 7.54 × 10^-4 m. How would this look in usual decimal form?

options.feedback: You moved the decimal 3 places, but the exponent is −4. You need one more jump!
Brilliant — this is your zone.
A negative exponent means the number is very small. You moved the decimal to the right to make a large number instead of moving it left.
The negative exponent tells you the number is small, not that the whole value is negative.

hint: A negative 4 exponent means you move the decimal 4 places to the left from its current spot.
full_solution: Starting at 7.54, move the decimal 4 places left: 0.000754.

title: The Computer Chip
question_text: A wire on a computer chip is 3 × 10^-8 m wide. What is this in usual form?

options.feedback: You nailed it — keep this momentum going.
Check your zero count. Moving 8 places left means you should have 7 zeros after the decimal point before the 3.
You created a huge number! Remember, 10^-8 means the number is a tiny fraction.
Extra shift.

hint: Take the number 3 (which is 3.0) and move the decimal 8 places to the left.
full_solution: Moving the decimal 8 places left from 3.0 results in 0.00000003.

title: Usual Suspects

options.feedback: Great job! That’s solid reasoning.

options.text: False, the correct answer isnt in the options.
options.feedback: If you move the decimal 6 places left from 3.02, you land exactly on 0.00000302. Try counting the jumps!
A negative exponent means move left for smallness, not right for largeness!
Exponents are position markers, not numbers to multiply by the base.

hint: Move the decimal point 6 places to the left.
full_solution: Moving the decimal 6 places left from 3.02 requires adding 5 zeros after the decimal point. The statement is True.

title: The Bacteria Build

options.feedback: This would be a giant bacteria! Negative exponents are for tiny things.
Spot on — you’re improving fast.
Almost! But standard form only allows the single digit 5 to be in front.
We use powers of 10, not multiplying the base by the exponent.

hint: Count how many places you move the decimal to the right to get behind the 5.
full_solution: Moving the decimal 7 places right gives 5 × 10^-7.

title: The Plant Cell Puzzle
question_text: The size of a plant cell is 0.00001275 m. What is this in standard form?

options.feedback: Well done — that was a sharp call.
You moved the decimal too far! Standard form requires exactly one non-zero digit before the decimal point.
Positive exponents are for large distances. For a microscopic cell, we need that negative exponent!
Not quite standard form yet! You need a non-zero digit (like 1) in front of the decimal.

hint: Move the decimal point so it sits right after the first non-zero digit (1).
full_solution: In 0.00001275, the decimal moves 5 places right to get to 1.275. Result: 1.275 × 10^-5 m.

title: The Electron’s Charge
question_text: The charge of an electron is 0.000, 000, 000, 000, 000, 000, 16 coulombs. How is this written in standard form?

options.feedback: Impressive — you’re thinking like a pro.
10^19 is a massive number! An electron's charge is incredibly tiny, so the exponent must be negative.
Check your placement! Standard form must have the decimal after the 1, not the 6.
Don't multiply 10 by the exponent! Use the exponent as a power.

hint: Count every single place the decimal jumps to the right until it is between the 1 and the 6.
full_solution: It takes 19 moves to the right to place the decimal between 1 and 6. Result: 1.6 × 10^-19.

title: Paper Thickness Redux
question_text: A student measures a stack of thick paper and finds a single sheet is 0.07 mm thick. What is the standard form of this thickness in meters? (Note: 1 mm = 0.001 m)

options.feedback: Impressive — you’re thinking like a pro.
This is the standard form in millimeters (0.07 mm = 7 × 10^-2 mm). You forgot to convert to meters!
This would mean the paper is 700,000 meters thick! Stick with negative exponents for small decimals.
Standard form requires the 7 to be in front of the decimal.

hint: First, convert 0.07 mm to meters by dividing by 1000 (or moving the decimal 3 more places left). Then convert that tiny decimal to standard form.
full_solution: First convert mm to m: 0.07 ÷ 1000 = 0.00007 m., Then move decimal 5 places right to get 7. Finally you get the result as 7 × 10^-5 m.
